text: @joshsuko8185  How was this game... made? Lol -- is it just 3D renderings and then the tracks were layered over top? Did the renderings include the tracks and then the engine kinda had to map what the rendering was showing with what the game considered to be "track"?
author: @Daywalkr

text: @Daywalkr  So it was a 3d pre-rendered track animation with no cars on it.  We would just manually animate the camera to follow the tracks.  Then the cars where real-time 3D and placed over the rendered track background.  I think there was also a real-time but invisible version of the track pieces that acted as a collision guide for the cars.  But I'm not 100% sure on that last point.

text:
@joshsuko8185 If you’re still answering questions about this game do you have any insight into the general design philosophies behind the tracks?  You mentioned the house stage being based off a real house and the sandbox being inspired by BMX- aside from the real house, would you guys usually create an area or room and then “run” track through it, so to speak, or was it usually the other way around and you’d build the scene around the track?  Also, aside from general kid-friendliness rules, were there any interesting rules or specific requests Mattel put on you when making the game?  Do you remember any other things (like the boiling pot/fishbowl swap) that got changed for the final product?

Sorry for the barrage of questions, it’s just cool being able to ask someone who worked on a game that’s such a core nostalgic memory for me and my brother 😁 we played the heck out of this game and its sequel when we were kids (and we’re in our mid-20’s now), especially the custom track maker in the second one.  The pre-rendering trick also worked on us as well, up until we found the real time graphics in the custom tracks in 2, because then it finally clicked exactly why the main game looked and played differently!  I know his favorite track was the sandcastle in 1, and mine was the river/creek in 2 (the one where your car gets eaten by the fish).
author: @samvoves9377

text:
@ Hey glad you enjoyed it! I can’t remember anything else like the boiling water but I was bummed that we had to take it out because I had created the steam and bubbling water and I really liked how it looked.  Another thing I remember is the “hands” that build the track in the intro, they have no body. The arms just end outside of the video frame. 

As for design philosophy I would say it started with the space and the track came second. But as the track was built little by little we would sometimes modify the environment to accommodate an idea that came up. So while the track was being built the scene was evolving with it. When I built the sandcastle level the track wasn’t planned out at the beginning. I just started building it and kept asking myself “what would feel cool next?” 

If I remember right there was some discussion about the endings and whether it felt like you actually “won” if your car got eaten or something like that.

text: I found this game fun for a while as a kid, but kinda limited.  I didn't understand that it was a video that would play out the same every time, but also that where you are versus where it looks like you are was never very in sync, so I got tripped up by this vagueness quite a bit.  Would've been nifty if it used a real 3D engine like quake engine (quake rally came out the year before), maybe somewhat improved in various ways kinda like how half life 1 was an enhanced quake engine.  Probably couldn't have the view of the big open room and other parts of the track without it being like a skybox or something, but I probably would've been happier with an uglier but more visually connected to what was going on game.  Also a 3D game would've been much more moddable.  The track editor I found to be especially disappointing.

text: All these years later, and this game in my opinion still has leagues better art direction and feel than Unleashed. The tracks in this game actually look super home-y, like how we'd make weird tracks and even use random objects in the house in some cases when we were kids. Unleashed has a way more uncanny vibe where the tracks look like they're made in real places but the level of detail is severely lacking and it's so lame in comparison. I wish Unleashed had a way more home-made heavy approach like the Stunt Track Driver games had. Even if these games aged, their charming art direction and OST really gave Hotwheels the kind of atmosphere it should have, replicating the nostalgic feeling of our childhoods, making goofy home-made tracks and having a unique OST to each map/setting. The fact Unleashed doesn't even have a unique OST to fit each map setting is depressing compared to this game where every map is vastly unique in presentation and music, even the track pieces used on the maps are somewhat more unique as well in terms of color choices or unique pieces. I might be a boomer but I donno, Unleashed feels more lifeless compared to something like this even if the gameplay didn't age the greatest, the atmosphere completely makes up for it.
author: @KasaneTetoPD2

text: I definitely think Unleashed and Unleashed 2's biggest setbacks are the lack of any kind of memorable music. text: I have such a distinct memory of finding the hidden level at the end. I had already had this game quite a while and all levels unlocked for a long time. My cousin happened to be over one day when we were playing it, and were fighting over the mouse on the level selection screen. Somehow I accidentally right-clicked right on the correct spot (the second T in the MATTEL logo) and unlocked the level I previously didn’t know existed. My cousin was pretty meh over the discovery but I lost my shit. I remember thinking how it looked like some futuristic alien landscape. Come to find out it’s just some unrendered polygons colored green 😂. But hey I guessed that’s what passed for “futuristic” back then eh? Good times.

text:
I'd just been browsing through Steam and ended up on your channel, and discovered this here video.
Ohhhh this game, back when I was younger and never cared about lag thinking it was a "slow-mo" effect, and graphics were completely ignored if the game was fun. And it was. I distinctly remember all the tricks you could do, how flips had seemingly infinite velocity, the insane tricks you could do on the sandbox level, crashing all the time, making pointless levels, unlocking new cars, never beating the last level, the useless cars, and of course little boy shnowshner turning off the lights quickly every time the fireworks played. Nowadays the game looks so blocky, buggy, slow and easy you can't help but wonder how you loved this game, but also know exactly why you did; we never cared. And that secret dev texture level would've killed me if I'd found it back then.
Being the Hot Wheels fan I was, this game had so many hours on me, beaten out only by Roller Coaster Tycoon which could keep me for 8+ hours straight. Only difference is that I can play RCT today, but this game sadly runs even worse and can crash more than just the 'wreck your car' way ;c
Man, if there is someone who knows how to get this game working on Windows 7, I'd been the happiest man alive. also mario6493 plz marry me

text:
This game is absolutly fantastic, so unqiue and fun, so many great memories!

I do think it's weird how we've all grown up though. If this game was released today it'd get 1 out of 10 probably. I think it's very weird how we used to tolerate extremely short, 2d, or poor blocky graphical games. I know we knew no better, and those were the best available at the time, but nothing was ever horrible to play. Yet this would be horrible to play today if you get me? Only because we enjoyed it so much back when we were young and have all these memories, you can tolerate the game and gameplay because you know deep down it's so much fun and makes you feel special inside and young again.

I love old games, games don't need to be 15+ hours to be considered good, just simple short sweet but extremely fun games, i've probably clocked more than 15+ hours going back playing these games repeatively because they are just simply so much fun!
